my_sentence: Yeah, I rest at home.
correction: I rested at home.
explanation: Talking about earlier today (finished action) takes the simple past: "I rested," not "I rest." The teacher modeled "I rested at home."
correct_examples: I rested at home today.
Yesterday I stayed at home and rested.
context: How he spent his day.

my_sentence: I eat some noodles for my dinner.
correction: I ate some noodles for dinner.
explanation: The meal already happened, so use the past "ate." Also "for dinner" is more natural than "for my dinner."
correct_examples: I ate some noodles for dinner.
She ate rice and vegetables for lunch.
context: What he had for dinner.

my_sentence: That can help us become a good citizen.
correction: That can help us become good citizens.
explanation: The plural subject "us" needs a plural complement: "good citizens," not "a good citizen."
correct_examples: Learning rules helps us become good citizens.
These lessons help children become responsible adults.
context: Why schools should teach practical life skills.

my_sentence: We need to respect others and that can keep our life more better.
correction: That can make our lives much better.
explanation: "Better" is already comparative, so "more better" is wrong — intensify it with "much better." The teacher confirmed "much better."
correct_examples: Following the rules makes our lives much better.
This new job is much better than the old one.
context: Benefits of learning rules and laws.

my_sentence: Just like a traffic rule, if someone try to break a red light...
correction: If someone tries to run a red light...
explanation: "Someone" is singular, so the verb takes -s: "tries," not "try." The teacher corrected "Tries to. If someone tries to."
correct_examples: If someone tries to run a red light, it causes accidents.
If anyone breaks the rule, they will be fined.
context: Examples of practical traffic rules.

my_sentence: If someone tries to break a red light...
correction: If someone tries to run a red light...
explanation: The collocation is "run a red light" (drive through it); "break a red light" sounds like physically smashing it. You "break a rule" but "run a red light." (assistant note)
correct_examples: He ran a red light and got a ticket.
Never run a red light, even when the road looks empty.
context: Examples of practical traffic rules.

my_sentence: It will cause a serious traffic jam.
correction: It can cause a serious traffic jam.
explanation: For a general possibility (not a certain future event), "can" fits better than "will." The teacher recast it: "No, it can cause a serious traffic jam."
correct_examples: Running a red light can cause a serious traffic jam.
Bad weather can cause delays.
context: Consequences of breaking traffic rules.

my_sentence: I haven't give my smartphone to my child.
correction: I don't give my smartphone to my child. / I haven't given my smartphone to my child.
explanation: After "haven't" use the past participle "given," or switch to the simple present for a habit: "I don't give." Jack self-corrected to "I don't give" after the teacher's prompt.
correct_examples: I don't give my smartphone to my daughter.
I have never given her a smartphone.
context: Whether he gives his smartphone to his child.

my_sentence: I think she is so young to use a smartphone.
correction: She is too young to use a smartphone.
explanation: Before "to + verb" expressing excess, use "too," not "so": "too young to use." "So" would need "that": "so young that she can't use it." (assistant note)
correct_examples: She is too young to use a smartphone.
He is too tired to keep working.
context: Why his daughter doesn't get a smartphone.

my_sentence: If you run out the limit, the smartphone can't work.
correction: If you use up the time limit, the smartphone stops working.
explanation: "Run out" needs "of" ("run out of time"), and what's exhausted is the time, not the limit. "Use up the limit" or "reach the limit" is clearer. (assistant note)
correct_examples: When she uses up her screen time, the phone locks itself.
If you run out of time, the app closes automatically.
context: Explaining smartphone screen-time limits.

my_sentence: She needs to protect her eyesight, so she can't use smartphone too much time.
correction: She shouldn't use the smartphone for too long.
explanation: "Too much time" can't directly modify "use the smartphone" — say "for too long" or "spend too much time on the smartphone." Also "the smartphone" needs an article. (assistant note)
correct_examples: She shouldn't use the smartphone for too long.
Kids spend too much time on their phones.
context: Limiting his daughter's screen time.

my_sentence: They need to know something they can do and something can't.
correction: They need to know the things they can do and the things that they can't.
explanation: The second half needs its relative pronoun and subject: "something that they can't (do)," not "something can't." The teacher corrected "Something that they can't."
correct_examples: Children should know what they can do and what they can't.
There are things that we can change and things that we can't.
context: Teaching children about rights and rules.

my_sentence: They don't know that's difficult to make money.
correction: They won't know it's difficult to make money.
explanation: For a future consequence, use "won't know." And the dummy subject in the clause is "it": "it's difficult to make money," not "that's difficult." The teacher supplied "They won't know. It's difficult to make."
correct_examples: They won't know it's difficult to make money.
Kids won't understand how hard earning money is until they try.
context: Why children should learn about money.

my_sentence: I think these skills need to learn from their parents.
correction: These skills need to be learned from the parents.
explanation: Skills don't learn — they are learned. After "need to," use the passive "be learned" when the subject receives the action. The teacher corrected "need to be learned."
correct_examples: These skills need to be learned from the parents.
Good habits need to be taught early.
context: Where practical life skills should come from.

my_sentence: Sometimes they went to the field, only me stay at home. I need to prepare food for myself.
correction: I needed to prepare food for myself, so I could learn to cook.
explanation: The childhood story is in the past, so keep the past tense: "I needed to," not "I need to." The teacher prompted "I needed."
correct_examples: When my parents worked in the field, I needed to cook for myself.
I stayed home alone and had to prepare my own food.
context: Practical skills learned during childhood.

my_sentence: There are many seeds can be planted.
correction: There are many seeds that can be planted.
explanation: After "there are many + noun," a following verb needs the relative pronoun "that": "seeds that can be planted." The same slip happened again with "many things we can harvest" → "there are many things that we can harvest."
correct_examples: There are many seeds that can be planted in spring.
There are many things that we can harvest in fall.
context: What can be planted in spring / harvested in fall.

my_sentence: Many of them will be die during that time.
correction: Many of them will die during that time. / Many people usually die whenever they try it.
explanation: "Die" is an active verb: "will die," never "will be die." The same error came back later ("many people will be die" about paragliding), where the teacher also recast "during this time" as "whenever they try this."
correct_examples: Many plants die in winter and recover in spring.
People sometimes die when they try extreme sports.
context: Plants dying in winter; paragliding accidents.

my_sentence: We use the coal to instead firewood.
correction: We use coal instead of firewood.
explanation: "Instead" is not a verb — use the preposition phrase "instead of + noun." The teacher: "'to' and 'instead' are not to be put together." Same pattern repeated later ("natural gas instead of it" was then correct).
correct_examples: We use coal instead of firewood.
She drinks tea instead of coffee.
context: How families cook without firewood.

my_sentence: We use the call... C-O-A-L. (intended: coal)
correction: coal /koʊl/
explanation: "Coal" /koʊl/ was pronounced like "call" /kɔːl/, and the teacher couldn't identify the word until it was spelled out. Practice the /oʊ/ diphthong: coal, cold, goal vs. call, tall.
correct_examples: We use coal for heating in winter.
China has many coal mines.
context: Talking about coal replacing firewood.

my_sentence: If you want to use firewood, you need to cut a lot of tree.
correction: You need to cut a lot of trees.
explanation: After "a lot of" with a countable noun, use the plural: "a lot of trees." The teacher repeated "Trees. Trees."
correct_examples: You need to cut a lot of trees to get firewood.
They planted a lot of trees along the road.
context: Why people switched from firewood to coal.

my_sentence: I think we will have more, we could have more.
correction: We have more.
explanation: For a present fact (China has more coal mines now), use the simple present "we have more" — not "will have" or "could have," which make it sound future or hypothetical. The teacher: "We have more, you should say."
correct_examples: We have more coal mines than the Philippines.
China has more than 4,000 active coal mines.
context: Comparing coal mines in China and the Philippines.

my_sentence: The place of coal mine can be found.
correction: The places where coal mines can be found.
explanation: Use the relative adverb "where" to describe a place: "the places where coal mines can be found," not "the place of coal mine can be found." The teacher explained: "we use the relative pronoun where."
correct_examples: Devices help locate the places where coal mines can be found.
This is the town where I grew up.
context: How countries find coal deposits.

my_sentence: They can learn to use their time by themselves. They don't need to teach by others.
correction: They don't need to be taught by others.
explanation: The students receive the teaching, so the passive is needed: "be taught by others," not "teach by others." The teacher modeled "They don't need to be told by others."
correct_examples: Students don't need to be taught by others how to relax.
Some skills don't need to be taught — they come naturally.
context: Whether students should be taught time management.

my_sentence: They can't just like machine to study all day.
correction: They are not machines that study all day.
explanation: The comparison needs a full clause: "they are not machines," not "they can't just like machine." The teacher confirmed "They are not machines."
correct_examples: Students are not machines that can study all day.
We are not robots — we need rest too.
context: Arguing students need free time.

my_sentence: Just like us to work in the company.
correction: Just like us who work in the company.
explanation: To describe "us," use a relative clause: "us who work in the company," not the infinitive "us to work." The teacher corrected "Just like us who work."
correct_examples: Students need rest, just like us who work in companies.
People who work all day also need breaks.
context: Comparing students' need for rest to working adults.

my_sentence: My wife and me brought my daughter for hiking.
correction: My wife and I brought our daughter hiking. / Me and my wife brought our daughter to hike.
explanation: In subject position use "I," not "me": "My wife and I." Since both parents are the subject, the daughter is "our daughter," not "my daughter." Also "brought her hiking / to hike" rather than "for hiking."
correct_examples: My wife and I brought our daughter hiking many times.
My wife and I took our daughter to the mountains.
context: Family hiking trips.

my_sentence: She was so young and can't work a lot, so I had to carry her up. (intended: walk)
correction: She was so young and couldn't walk a lot, so I had to carry her.
explanation: "Walk" /wɔːk/ came out as "work" /wɜːrk/, so the teacher asked "Couldn't work?" — the reverse of the same walk/work confusion from the previous class. Also keep past tense: "couldn't," not "can't."
correct_examples: She couldn't walk very far, so I carried her.
My daughter walked a few miles and then got tired.
context: Hiking with his young daughter.

my_sentence: We did not go to the top, we just walk to... a few miles.
correction: We just walked for a few miles.
explanation: Past narrative needs the past form "walked," and distance takes "for": "walked for a few miles." The teacher modeled the full sentence.
correct_examples: We just walked for a few miles.
They hiked for two hours and turned back.
context: Describing a family hike.

my_sentence: For me, I think it's too tired and exhausted.
correction: It's too tiring and exhausting.
explanation: Things that cause the feeling take -ing adjectives: the hike is "tiring and exhausting"; the person is "tired and exhausted." Jack self-corrected after starting with the -ed forms.
correct_examples: Climbing that mountain is too tiring and exhausting.
I felt tired because the trip was so exhausting.
context: Whether he wants to hike a steep mountain.

my_sentence: If my daughter wants to go here, I will company her.
correction: I will accompany her.
explanation: The verb is "accompany"; "company" is a noun. Jack self-corrected with the teacher's confirmation.
correct_examples: If my daughter wants to go, I will accompany her.
He accompanied his parents to the hospital.
context: Whether he would climb a mountain with his daughter.

my_sentence: More and more mountains start building their elevators for other peoples.
correction: ...for other people.
explanation: "People" is already plural — "peoples" only means "ethnic groups/nations." The teacher: "not peoples, sir, it's already the plural."
correct_examples: They built elevators for people who can't walk far.
The park added facilities for older people.
context: Mountain elevators for tourists in China.

my_sentence: I have some camping with my family.
correction: I went camping with my family.
explanation: The activity collocation is "go camping" (go + -ing activity): "I went camping," not "I have some camping." Same pattern: go hiking, go shopping, go fishing.
correct_examples: I went camping with my family last spring.
We often go camping at a countryside park.
context: Camping experiences with family.

my_sentence: During weekends or holidays there are a lot of people will go there.
correction: There are a lot of people who go there for camping.
explanation: "A lot of people" plus another verb needs the relative pronoun "who": "people who go there," not "people will go there." Same missing-relative pattern as "seeds can be planted" earlier in the day.
correct_examples: There are a lot of people who go camping on weekends.
There are many families who camp at the beach in summer.
context: Popular camping spots on weekends.

my_sentence: You need to be there in the early morning.
correction: You need to be there early in the morning.
explanation: The set phrase is "early in the morning." "In the early morning" exists but the teacher's target here was the adverbial "early in the morning" word order.
correct_examples: You need to go there early in the morning to get a spot.
We left early in the morning to avoid traffic.
context: Getting a camping spot before the crowds.

my_sentence: We usually go camping two weeks a time.
correction: We usually go camping every two weeks.
explanation: For frequency, say "every two weeks" or "once every two weeks." "Two weeks a time" mixes it up with "at a time," which describes duration, not frequency. (assistant note)
correct_examples: We go camping every two weeks in spring.
They visit their parents once every two weeks.
context: How often the family goes camping.

my_sentence: 5G network can cover most of city areas.
correction: 5G can cover most of the city areas.
explanation: "Most of" before a specific noun needs "the": "most of the city areas" (or drop "of": "most city areas"). The teacher corrected "Most of the city areas."
correct_examples: 5G covers most of the city areas.
Most of the parks in my city are free.
context: 5G coverage in China.

my_sentence: The company spent more than trillion yuan to build their 5G network.
correction: These companies spent more than a trillion yuan to build their 5G network.
explanation: Large numbers need the article: "a trillion," "a million," "a hundred." The teacher prompted "More than a trillion yuan."
correct_examples: They spent more than a trillion yuan on the network.
The project cost over a billion dollars.
context: Investment in China's 5G network.

my_sentence: I think it belonged to the government.
correction: It belongs to the government.
explanation: A present fact takes the present tense: "belongs," not "belonged." The teacher: "It belongs to the government, not its belong. It belongs, sir."
correct_examples: The telecom companies belong to the government.
This building belongs to the university.
context: Who owns China's cellular network companies.

my_sentence: Some companies provide electric energy.
correction: Some companies provide electrical energy.
explanation: The adjective for energy/power systems is "electrical": "electrical energy." "Electric" describes devices powered by electricity (electric car, electric stove).
correct_examples: Power plants provide electrical energy to the city.
They generate electrical energy from coal and natural gas.
context: Companies that generate electricity in China.

my_sentence: I visited more than twenties fast food restaurant in Yantai.
correction: I visited more than 20 fast food restaurants in Yantai.
explanation: The number stays "twenty" ("twenties" is only for ages/decades), and the counted noun is plural: "20 fast food restaurants." The teacher also drilled the answer pattern for "How many X have you visited?" — subject + verb + number + plural noun.
correct_examples: I visited more than 20 fast food restaurants in Yantai.
He ate three apples.
context: Answering "How many fast food restaurants have you visited?"

my_sentence: In my city, Kfc provide a lot of local food.
correction: KFC provides a lot of local food.
explanation: A company name is a singular subject, so the verb takes -s: "KFC provides." Jack self-corrected after the teacher's prompt "Provides."
correct_examples: KFC provides a lot of local dishes in China.
The restaurant serves both breakfast and dinner.
context: Local food sold at KFC in China.

my_sentence: For me, I'm a member, I'm a membership.
correction: I have a membership. / I'm a member.
explanation: You "have a membership" or "are a member" — you can't "be a membership." The teacher corrected "I have a membership."
correct_examples: I have a membership, so delivery is free.
She is a member of the gym.
context: Free delivery through a membership.

my_sentence: Kfc and other companies, they become a union.
correction: KFC and other companies unite. / KFC partners with other companies.
explanation: For companies cooperating, use the verb "unite" or "partner with" — "become a union" suggests forming a labor union. The teacher: "Unite... KFC and other companies unite."
correct_examples: KFC and JD partner to offer joint memberships.
The two companies united to share customers.
context: Explaining the KFC–JD joint membership.

my_sentence: I spend 158 yuan to register a membership of Jingdong.
correction: I spent 158 yuan to apply for a membership on JD.
explanation: The collocation is "apply for a membership" (or "sign up for"), not "register a membership." Also past tense "spent" for a finished purchase.
correct_examples: I applied for a JD membership for 158 yuan a year.
You can sign up for a membership online.
context: How he got the joint membership.

my_sentence: They gave me it for free.
correction: They gave it to me for free.
explanation: When the direct object is a pronoun ("it"), put it first: "gave it to me," not "gave me it."
correct_examples: They gave it to me for free.
She sent it to us yesterday.
context: Getting the KFC membership for free.

my_sentence: No one wants to invest them.
correction: No one wants to invest in them.
explanation: "Invest" takes "in" before the target: "invest in them," not "invest them." The teacher corrected "In them, in them."
correct_examples: No one wants to invest in top-floor restaurants.
He invested in a small coffee shop.
context: Investing in top-floor restaurants.

my_sentence: For rich people, their time is expensive so they need to reduce time waste.
correction: They need to reduce their time consumption. / They can't afford to waste time.
explanation: "Reduce time waste" is awkward; say "reduce their time consumption" (teacher's version) or more naturally "avoid wasting time."
correct_examples: Rich people can't afford to waste time commuting.
Living downtown reduces their time consumption.
context: Why rich people live near the city center.

my_sentence: There are some coffee stores on the top floor... some coffee areas.
correction: Coffee shops.
explanation: The standard term is "coffee shop" (or café) — not "coffee store" or "coffee area." The teacher: "Coffee shops, sir."
correct_examples: There are some coffee shops on the top floor.
We met at a coffee shop near the office.
context: Businesses on the top floors of buildings.

my_sentence: There are a lot of people in a neighborhood so they can provide a lot of service for us.
correction: They can provide a lot of services for us.
explanation: Countable "service" (kinds of service offered) takes the plural after "a lot of": "a lot of services." The teacher: "Services, services."
correct_examples: Dense neighborhoods can support a lot of services.
The building provides many services for residents.
context: Why delivery is cheap in Chinese apartment neighborhoods.

my_sentence: They can earn more money from them... They can earn more money from them... (repeating the same sentence several times)
correction: Say the sentence once, then add new information.
explanation: The teacher flagged this twice: "Your repetition, sir, you keep on repeating" and at the end "your repetitive errors... use less of that." Instead of restarting the same sentence, pause, then continue with the next idea.
correct_examples: They can earn a lot from the elevators. For example, one ride costs 50 to 100 yuan.
Delivery is free for members. That's why I order so often.
context: Recurring habit noted across the whole lesson.

my_sentence: We can buy them on the market.
correction: We can buy them in the market.
explanation: For a physical marketplace, say "in/at the market." "On the market" means "available for sale" (e.g. a house on the market). The teacher corrected "in the market."
correct_examples: You can buy coal in the market.
We bought fresh vegetables at the market.
context: Where people buy coal.

my_sentence: We can find some methods to reduce our stress. (heard as: distress)
correction: our stress — two words, not "distress"
explanation: "Our stress" was blended together so it sounded like "distress." The teacher: "Our stress, not distress." Keep a clear boundary between "our" and "stress."
correct_examples: We can find some methods to reduce our stress.
Exercise helps reduce our stress.
context: How workers relax after work.

my_sentence: For some disabled people they can go to the top to finish their dream.
correction: They can go to the top to fulfill their dreams.
explanation: Dreams are "fulfilled" (or "achieved"), not "finished." The teacher corrected "To fulfill, fulfill their dreams."
correct_examples: The elevator helps disabled people fulfill their dreams of reaching the top.
She worked hard to fulfill her dream of studying abroad.
context: Mountain elevators helping disabled visitors.

my_sentence: I think I don't know something about food industry.
correction: I don't know anything about the food industry.
explanation: In negative sentences, "something" becomes "anything": "don't know anything." Also "the food industry" needs the article. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I don't know anything about the food industry.
She doesn't know anything about cars.
context: Being asked about the food industry.

my_sentence: They do live a very luxury apartment building.
correction: They live in luxurious apartment buildings.
explanation: The adjective is "luxurious"; "luxury" is a noun (though it appears in fixed compounds like "luxury car"). The teacher recast it as "they live in luxurious buildings." Also note the missing "in" after "live."
correct_examples: Some rich people live in luxurious apartment buildings.
They stayed at a luxurious hotel.
context: Where rich people live in China.

my_sentence: You see some the seizure that build, build for drama. They were build for drama.
correction: They were built for a drama.
explanation: The passive needs the past participle "built," not the base form "build": "were built." The teacher confirmed "that were built."
correct_examples: Those restaurants were built for a TV drama.
The set was built inside a studio.
context: Buildings constructed as TV-drama sets.

my_sentence: I think it's not hard for deliver delivering food.
correction: It's not hard to deliver food.
explanation: After "it's (not) hard," use the infinitive: "hard to deliver," not "hard for deliver" or "hard for delivering." The teacher insisted: "to deliver. It's not hard to deliver food."
correct_examples: It's not hard to deliver food in dense neighborhoods.
It's easy to order food online here.
context: Food delivery to residential houses.

my_sentence: It's ten millions, more than ten millions yuan for one floor.
correction: It's more than ten million yuan for one floor.
explanation: After a number, "million" stays singular: "ten million yuan." "Millions" is only used alone, as in "millions of people." (assistant note)
correct_examples: The whole floor costs more than ten million yuan.
Millions of tourists visit the mountain every year.
context: Price of buying a whole apartment floor.
